title: AI Uncovers Over 100 Exoplanets, Including Rare Worlds in the 'Neptunian Desert'
summary: What if a computer could sift through millions of stars and pick out the ones hiding planets — even the weird, rare ones that break the rules? That's exactly what a team at the University of Warwick has done. They built an AI system named RAVEN and let it loose on four years of data from NASA's Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS). The result? A treasure trove of 118 newly validated exoplanets, plus over 2,000 high-quality candidates. Among them are some of the most extreme worlds ever found. TESS spots planets by catching the tiny dip in a star's brightness when a planet passes in front of it. But that dip can be faked by other cosmic events, like eclipsing binary stars. RAVEN cuts through the noise. It was trained on hundreds of thousands of realistic simulations — both real planet transits and imposters — so it learned to recognize the subtle patterns that separate genuine signals from false ones. 'The challenge lies in identifying if the dimming is indeed caused by a planet in orbit around the star or by something else,' said Dr. Andreas Hadjigeorghiou, who led RAVEN's development. 'Its strength stems from our carefully created dataset of hundreds of thousands of realistically simulated planets.' The new batch of planets includes some head-scratchers. Ultra-short-period planets orbit their stars in less than a day — imagine a year that lasts just hours. Others reside in the so-called 'Neptunian desert,' a region close to stars where planets of Neptune's size are mysteriously scarce. Finding them there challenges current theories of how planets form and survive. RAVEN also revealed tightly packed multi-planet systems, including previously unknown pairs of worlds circling the same star. Beyond the discoveries, the team used this clean dataset to measure how common close-in planets really are. In a companion study, they found that about 9–10% of Sun-like stars host a planet with an orbit shorter than 16 days. That aligns with earlier results from NASA's Kepler mission, but with up to ten times less uncertainty. 'For the first time, we can put a precise number on just how empty this desert is,' said Dr. Kaiming Cui, who led the population study. The team has released interactive catalogs and tools so astronomers worldwide can follow up on the most promising worlds with ground-based telescopes or future missions like ESA's PLATO. RAVEN marks a new era where AI doesn't just speed up discovery — it makes the results reliable enough to answer big questions about our galaxy.
why_it_matters: This breakthrough shows how artificial intelligence is not just speeding up science but making it more reliable — helping us understand how common planets like Earth might be, and what extreme places our galaxy holds.

keywords.explanation: Planets that orbit stars outside our own solar system.
A method used to find planets by measuring the tiny dip in a star's brightness when a planet passes in front of it.
Two stars that orbit each other and block each other's light, which can sometimes mimic the signal of a planet.
Computer-generated fake data that mimics real observations, used to train the AI to tell real planets from false signals.
A region close to a star where Neptune-sized planets are mysteriously rare, challenging current theories of planet formation.
Planets that orbit their stars extremely fast, finishing a full year in less than a single Earth day.

questions.question: What is the name of the AI system that helped discover over 100 exoplanets?
How many exoplanets did RAVEN validate from TESS data?
What puzzling region near stars did some of the new planets belong to?
Roughly what percentage of Sun-like stars appear to have a planet with an orbit shorter than 16 days?
Who led the development of the RAVEN AI?
Which future space mission will use these catalogs to study some of the most promising worlds?

background_read: NASA's Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS, launched in 2018 to survey nearly the whole sky for planets) has been scanning millions of stars, looking for the tiny brightness dips that reveal planets. Before AI like RAVEN, scientists had to manually check thousands of these signals, many of which turned out to be false alarms from eclipsing binary stars.

The University of Warwick (a public research university in England) built on decades of exoplanet research that started in the 1990s. NASA's Kepler mission (a space telescope that discovered thousands of exoplanets from 2009-2018) already showed that small rocky planets are common, but its field of view was small. TESS widened the search to bright, nearby stars, creating an enormous dataset that required clever machine-learning tools.

Understanding the 'Neptunian desert' is important because Neptune-sized planets shouldn’t survive that close to their stars — yet some do. The European Space Agency's PLATO mission (planned for launch in 2026 to find and study Earth-like planets) will follow up many of RAVEN's finds, looking for atmospheres and signs of habitability.

The fact that about 1 in 10 Sun-like stars hosts a close-in planet adds a solid number to our picture of how planetary systems form. With less uncertainty than earlier estimates, astronomers can now refine computer simulations of planet birth and migration, piecing together the story of our own solar system’s history.
Article_Structure: LEAD: A hook that asks what if a computer could sift through millions of stars to find rare planets.
├─ WHAT: Researchers at the University of Warwick built an AI called RAVEN that validated 118 new exoplanets.
├─ HOW: TESS detects tiny brightness dips; RAVEN was trained on hundreds of thousands of real and fake planet transits.
KEY CHALLENGE: Telling real planet transits apart from eclipsing binary stars.
└─ QUOTE: Dr. Andreas Hadjigeorghiou explains the difficulty and highlights the huge simulated dataset.
DISCOVERIES: The new planets include extreme worlds.
├─ Ultra-short-period planets with years lasting hours.
├─ Planets in the Neptunian desert — a zone where such worlds shouldn’t exist.
└─ Tightly packed multi-planet systems, some newly recognized.
EVIDENCE: A companion population study finds 9–10% of Sun-like stars have a close-in planet.
└─ Much less uncertainty thanks to the clean AI-vetted data.
TOOLS & FUTURE: Interactive catalogs released for follow-up with ground-based telescopes or ESA’s PLATO mission.
OPEN QUESTION: What do these rare planets tell us about how planets form and migrate?

perspectives.description: RAVEN proves that AI can sift through massive astronomical data with far greater speed and accuracy than humans alone, significantly expanding the catalog of known worlds. This opens the door to finding Earth-like planets and understanding the true diversity of planetary systems.

Some astronomers worry that black-box AI might discard unusual but genuine signals that don’t match its training, potentially missing once-in-a-lifetime discoveries. A system trained on past data may also inherit hidden biases, skewing our picture of the cosmos.

perspectives.perspective: Neutral – Part of a Larger Shift in Astronomy
perspectives.description: The use of machine learning to handle survey data is now standard across science, following the footsteps of the Kepler mission. This study validates that AI can produce results as trustworthy as traditional, manual vetting, but with far less human effort, reflecting an inevitable trend.

perspectives.perspective: Forward-looking – Next Generation of Planet Hunting
perspectives.description: The clean dataset and catalogs will allow upcoming missions like PLATO and the James Webb Space Telescope to target the most promising worlds for atmospheric study. In the next decade, we may finally be able to tell whether any of these planets have conditions suitable for life.
